perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall remain in full force and effect until revoked by any party hereto in a signed writing provided to each other party hereto, and then only with respect to such revoking party.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall remain in effect until possibly revoked by any party hereto with notification to the other parties, subject to their interpretation of the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces significant ambiguity regarding the revocation process. The use of 'possibly revoked' and 'subject to their interpretation of the circumstances' creates uncertainty about whether a signed writing is required and whether other parties can prevent the revocation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 13 and 16
perturbation.law_explanation: The change contradicts the intent of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, specifically Section 13 and 16 (and related regulations like 17 CFR § 240.13d-1), which requires clear and unambiguous filings. The ambiguous revocation process makes it unclear when a party is no longer subject to the joint filing agreement, potentially leading to non-compliance with reporting requirements under these sections of the Act. For example, if one party believes it has revoked but the others disagree, it may fail to file required information, resulting in a violation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the revocation process of the agreement, using terms like 'possibly revoked' and 'subject to their interpretation of the circumstances.' This directly undermines the clarity required for filings under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, specifically Sections 13 and 16, as interpreted by the LLM's explanation. While the scraped snippets focus on filing requirements (17 CFR § 240.13d-1), the Law Explanation connects this ambiguity to potential non-compliance with reporting obligations, as it becomes unclear when a party is no longer subject to the joint filing agreement. The original text required 'signed writing,' which provides a clear record, whereas the changed text's 'interpretation of the circumstances' introduces subjectivity, violating the ambiguity definition requiring difficulty to enforce under law.

perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to and in accordance with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"), and the rules and regulations thereunder, each party hereto hereby agrees to the joint filing, on behalf of each of them, of any filing required by such party under Section 13 or Section 16 of the Exchange Act or any rule or regulation thereunder (including any amendment, restatement, supplement, and/or exhibit thereto) with the Securities and Exchange Commission (and, if such security is registered on a national securities exchange, also with the exchange), and further agrees to the filing, furnishing, and/or incorporation by reference of this agreement as an exhibit thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: In general alignment with the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"), and the rules and regulations thereunder, each party hereto expresses an intention to jointly file, on behalf of each of them, filings related to Section 13 or Section 16 of the Exchange Act or any rule or regulation thereunder (including any amendment, restatement, supplement, and/or exhibit thereto) with the Securities and Exchange Commission (and, if such security is registered on a national securities exchange, also with the exchange), and further agrees to consider the filing, furnishing, and/or incorporation by reference of this agreement as an exhibit thereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to the joint filing' to 'expresses an intention to jointly file' weakens the commitment to a mandatory obligation. Substituting 'agrees to consider the filing, furnishing, and/or incorporation by reference' further diminishes the binding nature of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 13 and 16
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity and weakens the legally binding nature of the agreement, which may contradict the requirements of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Section 13(d) and 16 of the Act, along with associated regulations like 17 CFR § 240.13d-1, necessitate precise and guaranteed joint filings by parties acting in concert. Introducing terms like 'intention' and 'consider' undermines the certainty required by the Act, potentially leading to parties not meeting their obligations and thus violating securities laws.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.13d-1 - Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.13d-1 Filing of Schedules 13D and 13G. (a) Any person who, after acquiring directly or indirectly the beneficial ownership of any equity security of a class which is specified in paragraph (i)(1) of this section , is directly or indirectly the beneficial owner of more than five percent of the class shall, within five business days after the date of the acquisition, file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) . (b) (1) A person who would otherwise be obligated under paragraph (a) of this section to file a statement on Schedule 13D (§ 240.13d-101) may, in lieu thereof, file with the Commission, a short-form statement on Schedule 13G (§ 240.13d-102), Provided, That: (i) Such person has acquired such securities in the ordinary course of the person's business and not with the purpose nor with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer , nor in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having such purpose or effect, including any transaction subject to § 240.13d-3(b) and (ii) Such person is: (A) A broker or dealer registered under section 15 of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78o ); (B) A bank as defined in section 3(a)(6) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (C) An insurance company as defined in section 3(a)(19) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78c ); (D) An investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-8 ); (E) Any person registered as an investment adviser under Section 203 of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 ( 15 U.S.C. 80b-3 ) or under the laws of any state; (F) An employee benefit plan as defined in Section 3(3) of the Employee Retirement Income Security A
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'agrees to the joint filing,' indicating a firm commitment, whereas the changed text uses 'expresses an intention to jointly file,' weakening the obligation. Similarly, 'agrees to consider the filing' further reduces the binding nature. The Law Explanation states that Section 13(d) and 16 necessitate 'precise and guaranteed joint filings.' The modified text undermines this certainty, potentially violating securities laws. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 CFR § 240.13d-1, emphasizes the filing requirements for beneficial owners of more than five percent of a class of equity security, mandating they 'file with the Commission, a statement containing the information required by Schedule 13D.' The change from 'agrees' to 'intention' and 'consider' weakens the obligatory nature of the filing, which contradicts the mandatory filing requirements under 17 CFR § 240.13d-1.
